title: Search Autocomplete — System Design
description: Designing search autocomplete (typeahead): as a user types a prefix, instantly suggest the most likely completions. The interesting problems are a read-heavy, latency-critical prefix lookup (every keystroke is a query), ranking suggestions by popularity, keeping suggestions fresh from the live query stream, and doing it for millions of QPS within tens of milliseconds. We build from a naive prefix scan toward a trie-backed, top-k-precomputed, cached, sharded suggestion service.

nodes.label: Popularity Aggregator
nodes.description: Popularity Aggregator: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Autocomplete API
nodes.description: Autocomplete API: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Index Builder
nodes.description: Index Builder: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.label: Edge / Prefix Cache
nodes.description: Edge / Prefix Cache: Network-edge component used for global delivery, caching, or traffic acceleration.

nodes.label: CDN
nodes.description: CDN: Network-edge component used for global delivery, caching, or traffic acceleration.

nodes.label: Client / Search Box
nodes.description: Client / Search Box: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Query Counts
nodes.description: Query Counts: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Query / Term DB
nodes.description: Query / Term DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Personalization Service
nodes.description: Personalization Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: User History
nodes.description: User History: Authoritative or operational state store for records, metadata, counters, locks, or durable state.

nodes.label: Prefix Router
nodes.description: Prefix Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Index Shard A–M
nodes.description: Index Shard A–M: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.label: Index Shard N–Z
nodes.description: Index Shard N–Z: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.label: Spell / Fuzzy Matcher
nodes.description: Spell / Fuzzy Matcher: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Query Event Stream
nodes.description: Query Event Stream: Append-only event stream, feed, bus, or log used for ordering, replay, or fan-out.

nodes.label: Precomputed Top-K per Prefix
nodes.description: Precomputed Top-K per Prefix: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.label: Prefix Index — Trie
nodes.description: Prefix Index — Trie: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

functional: Given a prefix, return the top suggestions that start with it.
Reflect newly trending queries within minutes.
Optionally personalize suggestions to the user.
nonFunctional: Very low latency: tens of milliseconds per keystroke.
Extremely read-heavy and high QPS (every keystroke queries).
Suggestions stay reasonably fresh as query popularity shifts.
Scale to a large vocabulary and global traffic.
Graceful degradation: return fewer/cached suggestions rather than fail.
Suggestions are eventually consistent — slight staleness is fine.

capacity.label: Query rate
capacity.note: Each keystroke is a request; reads dominate everything.

capacity.label: Latency target
capacity.note: It must feel instant as the user types.

capacity.label: Vocabulary
capacity.note: The set of completable strings is huge and long-tailed.

capacity.label: Suggestions returned
capacity.note: Only the best few per prefix matter.

capacity.label: Freshness
capacity.note: Trending terms should appear soon, but not instantly.

capacity.label: Read:write
capacity.note: Index updates are batched; reads are constant and hot.

api.description: Return ranked completions for a prefix. Aggressively cacheable; served from the edge when possible.
Record an executed search (feeds popularity). Fire-and-forget into the event stream.

dataModel.name: terms
dataModel.note: Completable strings with a popularity score.

dataModel.name: prefix_topk
dataModel.note: Precomputed top-k completions per prefix — the read-optimized structure.

dataModel.name: query_counts
dataModel.note: Rolling popularity counts aggregated from the query event stream.

dataModel.name: user_history
dataModel.note: Optional per-user recent/started queries for personalization.

patterns.name: Trie / prefix index
patterns.what: A tree keyed by characters so all completions of a prefix sit under one node.
patterns.whenToUse: Prefix lookups over a large vocabulary.

patterns.name: Precomputed top-k
patterns.what: Store the ranked best-k completions per prefix node so a query is a direct read, not a subtree scan.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy ranking where computing per query is too slow.

patterns.name: Edge caching
patterns.what: Cache popular prefixes near users (CDN/edge) since the same short prefixes are queried constantly.
patterns.whenToUse: High QPS with a heavily skewed prefix distribution.

patterns.name: Stream aggregation
patterns.what: Aggregate the live query stream into rolling popularity counts.
patterns.whenToUse: Deriving freshness/trends from a firehose of events.

patterns.name: Batch index rebuild (CQRS)
patterns.what: Rebuild the read-optimized index offline from counts, then swap it in atomically.
patterns.whenToUse: When the query structure is expensive to update in place.

patterns.name: Fuzzy matching
patterns.what: Tolerate typos via edit-distance or n-gram expansion of the prefix.
patterns.whenToUse: Real users mistype; exact prefix match alone misses intent.

patterns.name: Sharding by prefix
patterns.what: Partition the index by leading characters across nodes.
patterns.whenToUse: When the index or QPS outgrows one node.

steps.title: 1. Naive Prefix Scan (the baseline)
steps.description: Start with a table of terms and a LIKE 'prefix%' query at request time, sorted by popularity. It works for a small list; at 1M QPS over 100M terms with a tens-of-ms budget it's hopeless — which motivates a prefix index and precomputation.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why can't you run a 'LIKE prefix%' ORDER BY score query on every keystroke?

concepts.definition: Find all strings starting with a given prefix.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the core operation, run on literally every keystroke.

concepts.definition: Tens of ms end-to-end including network.
concepts.whyItMatters: Leaves almost no room for a scan-and-sort per request.

talkingPoints: Frame it as a read-heavy, latency-critical prefix problem.

traps.trap: Running LIKE 'prefix%' ORDER BY popularity per keystroke
traps.why: Scanning and ranking matching rows per request can't meet a tens-of-ms budget at 1M QPS.
traps.instead: Use a prefix index and precompute the top-k per prefix.

bottlenecks.issue: Per-request scan + sort over a huge term set.

strong: Identifies read-heaviness and the latency budget as the drivers.
weak: Thinks a DB LIKE query is fine at scale.

whyNow: The baseline makes the trie and precomputation obvious responses.

steps.title: 2. Trie Prefix Index
steps.description: Build a trie (prefix tree) keyed by characters: every completion of a prefix lives in the subtree under that prefix's node. Looking up a prefix becomes walking down the characters, turning a scan into an O(prefix length) traversal.
steps.decisionPrompt: How does a trie turn 'find all completions of a prefix' from a scan into a cheap lookup?

concepts.definition: A tree where each edge is a character and each path spells a prefix.
concepts.whyItMatters: All completions of a prefix share a subtree, so lookup is a short walk.

concepts.definition: Everything under a prefix node completes that prefix.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reduces 'which terms match' to a tree position.

options.name: Trie in memory (default)
pros: O(prefix length) lookup; compact with shared prefixes.
In-memory => microsecond traversal.
cons: Still must rank the subtree's completions (addressed by top-k).
Rebuild/update strategy needed.

options.name: Sorted list + binary search on prefix
pros: Dead simple; a prefix range is contiguous when sorted.
cons: Range can be huge for short prefixes; still must scan+rank the range.
Updates shift the array.

traps.trap: Walking the entire subtree to gather completions per query
traps.why: A short prefix like 's' has millions of completions — gathering them all per keystroke is slow.
traps.instead: Precompute and store the top-k at each node (next step), so lookup returns immediately.

traps.trap: Storing the trie only on disk
traps.why: Per-keystroke disk traversal blows the latency budget.
traps.instead: Keep the hot trie in memory; persist for rebuild.

failureDrills.scenario: A one-character prefix is queried heavily.
failureDrills.mitigation: Precomputed top-k at that node + caching make it a constant-time read.

strong: Chooses a trie and notes the subtree-ranking problem.

recap.after: A trie makes finding completions an O(prefix) walk.
recap.newRisk: Ranking the subtree's completions is still expensive — precompute it.

whyNow: The trie is the core index; top-k precomputation builds directly on it.

steps.title: 3. Precomputed Top-K per Prefix
steps.description: At each trie node (prefix), precompute and store the ranked top-k completions by popularity. A query then walks to the node and returns its stored list directly — no subtree scan or per-query ranking. This is the key read optimization.
steps.decisionPrompt: A prefix has a million completions but you show 8. How do you avoid ranking a million per keystroke?

concepts.definition: Each prefix node caches its best-k completions, precomputed offline.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a query into a single read instead of a scan + sort.

concepts.definition: Expensive ranking happens at build time; queries are cheap reads.
concepts.whyItMatters: Matches the workload — billions of reads, batched writes.

deepDives.title: Building and storing top-k
points: Compute each node's top-k bottom-up: a node's candidates are its children's top-k plus its own terminal term.
Store the k suggestions inline at the node so a lookup is one read.
k is small (5–10), so storage overhead per node is bounded.

traps.why: Sorting a large candidate set per keystroke blows the latency budget at 1M QPS.
traps.instead: Precompute top-k per node offline; the query just reads it.

traps.trap: Storing full completion lists at every node
traps.why: Wastes memory and isn't needed — only the top few are shown.

failureDrills.scenario: Popularity shifts so the precomputed top-k is stale.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Suggestions are slightly behind, not wrong-shaped.
failureDrills.mitigation: Periodic rebuild from fresh counts (freshness step); staleness of minutes is acceptable.

strong: Precomputes top-k bottom-up and reads it directly.

recap.after: Each prefix node returns its precomputed top-k in one read.
recap.newRisk: Top-k goes stale as popularity shifts; it must be rebuilt periodically.

whyNow: Precomputed top-k is what makes the read path fast enough.

options.name: Precompute top-k per node offline (default)
pros: A keystroke is one read of the node's stored list — no subtree scan or per-query ranking.
Matches the workload: ranking happens once at build time, billions of reads stay cheap.
k is tiny (5-10), so the per-node storage overhead is bounded.
cons: Top-k goes stale as popularity shifts; needs periodic rebuild from fresh counts.
Build is a full bottom-up pass over the trie, expensive for a 100M-term vocabulary.
Brand-new trending terms can't appear until the next rebuild.

options.name: Rank the subtree at query time
pros: Always reflects the current popularity scores — no rebuild lag at all.
No extra top-k structure to store or keep in sync with the trie.
cons: A short prefix like 's' has millions of completions to gather and sort per keystroke — blows the tens-of-ms budget at 1M QPS.
Work scales with subtree size, exactly the wrong shape for an extremely read-heavy load.
CPU cost per request is unbounded for popular short prefixes.

options.name: Sorted-set leaderboard per prefix
pros: A prefix maps to a Redis sorted set; reading the top-k is a single ZRANGE.
Scores update incrementally (ZADD) so popularity stays current without a full rebuild.
Off-the-shelf primitive — no custom top-k build pipeline.
cons: One sorted set per prefix multiplies key count enormously across a 100M-term vocabulary.
Short prefixes still hold huge sets, so the hot keys are large and memory-heavy.
Per-prefix sets duplicate completions that a shared trie subtree would store once.

flows.name: Prefix lookup -> top-k read
flows.note: A keystroke walks the trie to the prefix node and returns its precomputed top-k directly — no subtree scan or ranking.

steps.title: 4. Edge Caching
steps.description: Prefix queries are wildly skewed — short, common prefixes dominate. Cache suggestion responses at the edge/CDN and in a prefix cache so the hottest prefixes never reach the index. Short TTLs keep them fresh enough.
steps.decisionPrompt: The same few thousand short prefixes are queried constantly. How do you avoid hitting the index for them?

concepts.definition: A small set of prefixes accounts for most traffic.
concepts.whyItMatters: Caching the hot head absorbs the vast majority of QPS.

concepts.definition: Cache responses near users with a short expiry.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cuts latency and load while bounding staleness.

traps.trap: Sending every keystroke to the index
traps.why: The hot head of prefixes hammers the index needlessly when their answer rarely changes within seconds.
traps.instead: Cache suggestions at the edge with a short TTL; only misses hit the index.

traps.why: Trending terms never appear because the cache never refreshes.
traps.instead: Short TTL (seconds–minutes) balances freshness against hit rate.

failureDrills.mitigation: Edge cache serves the hot head; stale-while-revalidate covers a short outage.

strong: Exploits prefix skew with edge caching + short TTL.

recap.after: The hot head is served from cache; misses hit the precomputed index.
recap.newRisk: TTL trades freshness vs hit rate; cache must handle high fan-in.

whyNow: Caching is the cheapest way to absorb 1M QPS given the skew.

options.name: Edge/CDN cache with short TTL (default)
pros: The skewed hot head of short prefixes is served near the user — most QPS never reaches the index.
Cuts latency to a CDN round-trip and shields the origin.
Short TTL plus stale-while-revalidate bounds staleness while surviving brief index outages.
cons: TTL trades freshness against hit rate — too long and trending terms lag.
Per-user personalized suggestions can't be edge-cached (cache key has no user).
Cache key explosion if prefixes carry many query parameters.

options.name: Shared app-tier cache only (no CDN)
pros: One central cache holds a consistent hot set and is simple to invalidate on rebuild.
Works for personalized or auth-scoped responses the CDN can't key on.
No CDN contract or edge-config complexity to manage.
cons: Every keystroke still crosses the network to your origin region — higher latency than the edge.
The shared cache becomes a high-fan-in hotspot at 1M QPS.
Loses the geographic locality that absorbs traffic closest to users.

options.name: Client-side prefix cache
pros: Re-typing or backspacing reuses results the browser already has — zero network for those.
Removes a slice of QPS from every layer behind it.
Instant response for prefixes the user just visited.
cons: Only helps a single user's own re-queries — no cross-user sharing of the hot head.
Can't reflect trending terms until the local entry expires.
Limited memory on device caps how much can be cached.

flows.name: Edge cache hit vs miss
flows.note: The skewed hot head is served from the edge/CDN; only a miss falls through to the index.

steps.title: 5. Freshness from the Query Stream
steps.description: Keep suggestions current: executed searches flow into an event stream, an aggregator maintains rolling popularity counts, and the index is rebuilt (or incrementally updated) from those counts periodically and swapped in atomically.
steps.decisionPrompt: A new term suddenly trends. How does it start showing up in suggestions within minutes?

concepts.whyItMatters: They are the popularity signal that ranks suggestions and surfaces trends.

concepts.definition: Build a new top-k index offline, then switch reads to it in one step.
concepts.whyItMatters: Updates the read path without partial/inconsistent states.

options.name: Periodic batch rebuild + atomic swap (default)
pros: Simple, consistent; rebuild every few minutes from fresh counts.
Read path untouched during build, then swapped.
cons: Freshness limited to the rebuild interval.
Full rebuild cost for large vocab.

options.name: Incremental in-place updates
pros: Near-real-time freshness.
cons: Updating top-k lists in place under heavy reads is tricky and error-prone.
Risk of inconsistent reads mid-update.

traps.trap: Updating the live index in place under 1M QPS reads
traps.why: Concurrent mutation of top-k lists risks inconsistent or torn reads on the hot path.
traps.instead: Rebuild offline and swap atomically; reads always see a complete version.

traps.trap: Counting raw query volume without decay
traps.why: Yesterday's blockbuster outranks today's trend forever.
traps.instead: Use a time-decayed/windowed count so popularity reflects recent behavior.

failureDrills.scenario: The aggregator lags during a traffic spike.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Suggestions get a bit staler, never wrong-shaped.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reads keep serving the last good index; the rebuild catches up when the stream drains.

strong: Aggregates the stream into decayed counts and rebuilds+swaps atomically.
Keeps mutation off the read path.
weak: Mutates the live index in place.

recap.before: The index was static after build.
recap.after: Rolling counts from the query stream drive periodic atomic rebuilds.
recap.newRisk: Freshness is bounded by the rebuild interval; counts need decay.

whyNow: Without the stream feeding rebuilds, suggestions go stale and miss trends.

flows.name: Index update from the query stream
flows.note: Executed searches stream in, an aggregator updates decayed counts, and the builder rebuilds the top-k index and swaps it in atomically.

steps.title: 6. Typo Tolerance & Personalization
steps.description: Real users mistype and have individual intent. Add fuzzy matching (edit-distance / n-gram expansion) so 'recieve' still suggests 'receive', and optionally blend personalized suggestions from the user's own history with the global top-k.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user types 'systen' — how do you still suggest 'system design' without scanning everything?

concepts.definition: Match prefixes within a small number of character edits.
concepts.whyItMatters: Captures intent despite typos, which exact prefix match misses.

concepts.definition: Merge the user's history with global suggestions, weighted.
concepts.whyItMatters: Surfaces what this user likely means without ignoring global popularity.

deepDives.title: Fuzzy matching cheaply
points: Generate a few edit-distance-1 variants of the prefix and look each up — bounded extra work.
Or index n-grams so near-miss prefixes share keys; rank exact matches above fuzzy ones.
Blend personalization as a re-rank on top of global top-k, not a separate slow path.

traps.trap: Doing a global edit-distance search over the whole vocabulary
traps.why: Comparing the prefix to every term is far too slow for the latency budget.
traps.instead: Expand the prefix into a few bounded variants (or use n-gram keys) and look those up.

traps.trap: Replacing global suggestions entirely with personal history
traps.why: A user with little history then gets poor, narrow suggestions.
traps.instead: Blend/re-rank personal over global; fall back to global when history is thin.

failureDrills.mitigation: Personalization is a re-rank layer over global top-k; absence degrades gracefully.

strong: Bounds fuzzy matching via prefix variants/n-grams.
Treats personalization as a re-rank with graceful fallback.
weak: Replaces global suggestions with sparse personal data.

recap.after: Typo-tolerant matching and optional personalization layered on.
recap.newRisk: Fuzzy and personalization add latency/complexity — keep them bounded and optional.

whyNow: Quality features come after the fast, fresh core works.

options.name: Bounded edit-distance prefix expansion (default)
pros: Generate a few edit-distance-1 variants of the typed prefix and look each up — bounded extra work per keystroke.
Reuses the existing trie + top-k read path; no separate index to maintain.
Exact matches can be ranked above fuzzy ones so correct prefixes still win.
cons: Only catches small typos; two-edit errors slip through unless you widen the variant set.
Variant generation multiplies lookups, eating into the latency budget.
Transpositions and phonetic errors aren't well covered by raw edit distance.

options.name: N-gram index for fuzzy match
pros: Near-miss prefixes share n-gram keys, so candidates are retrieved by overlap rather than enumerated variants.
Handles transpositions and multiple edits more gracefully than fixed edit-distance-1.
Retrieval cost is roughly independent of the number of edits.
cons: A separate n-gram index to build, store, and keep in sync with the trie.
N-gram candidates need a re-rank/filter step to drop false positives.
Larger storage footprint than the prefix trie alone.

options.name: Global edit-distance scan over the vocabulary
pros: Conceptually simple: compare the prefix against every term and keep the closest.
Catches any typo regardless of edit count or position.
cons: Comparing against 100M terms per keystroke is hopeless under a tens-of-ms budget.
Cost scales with vocabulary size, the exact opposite of what the workload needs.
Cannot reuse the cheap trie walk at all.

steps.title: 7. Scaling by Prefix Sharding
steps.description: When the index or QPS outgrows one node, shard by leading characters across nodes, fronted by a prefix router and the edge cache. Replicate shards for the read load. Most traffic is still absorbed by caching; sharding handles index size and misses.
steps.decisionPrompt: The trie no longer fits on one machine and misses still total huge QPS. How do you split it?

concepts.definition: Partition the index by leading character(s) across nodes.
concepts.whyItMatters: A query routes to exactly one shard by its prefix; no fan-out.

concepts.definition: Multiple copies of a shard to serve read load.
concepts.whyItMatters: Autocomplete is read-only at query time — replicate freely.

options.name: Shard by leading characters + replicas (default)
pros: A prefix maps to one shard — no scatter-gather.
Read replicas scale QPS; the index need not fit one node.
cons: Skew: some leading letters are far more common; needs balancing.
Routing + rebuild coordination.

options.name: Replicate the whole index to every node
pros: No routing; any node answers any prefix.
Simple.
cons: Every node must hold the entire index — caps vocabulary by one machine's memory.

deepDives.title: Handling prefix skew
points: Letters/prefixes aren't uniform ('s','t' >> 'x','z') — balance by splitting hot ranges finer.
The edge cache absorbs the skewed hot head, so shards mostly serve the long tail.
Replicate hot shards more heavily than cold ones.

traps.trap: Sharding by a hash of the full query
traps.why: Then completions of one prefix scatter across all shards, forcing scatter-gather per keystroke.
traps.instead: Shard by leading characters so a prefix maps to a single shard.

traps.why: The 's' shard melts while the 'z' shard idles.
traps.instead: Split hot prefix ranges finer and replicate them more.

strong: Shards by prefix (no fan-out), replicates reads, balances skew.
Lets the cache absorb the hot head.

recap.before: One node held the whole index and all misses.
recap.after: Prefix-sharded, replicated index behind the cache scales size and QPS.
recap.newRisk: Prefix skew needs active balancing; rebuilds must coordinate across shards.

whyNow: Sharding is the final lever once the fast, fresh, cached core is built.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: keystrokes hit an edge/CDN cache that absorbs the skewed hot head; misses route by prefix to a sharded, replicated in-memory trie whose nodes hold precomputed top-k completions; executed searches stream into a popularity aggregator whose decayed counts drive periodic atomic index rebuilds; fuzzy matching and optional personalization re-rank on top.

functional.how: Trie walk to a node holding precomputed top-k.
Top-k built from decayed popularity counts.
Query stream -> rolling counts -> periodic rebuild.
Re-rank global top-k with user history.

nonFunctional.how: Edge cache + in-memory trie + precomputed top-k = constant-time reads.
Edge caching absorbs the hot head; prefix shards + replicas serve misses.
Stream aggregation drives periodic atomic rebuilds.
Cache (stale-while-revalidate) serves during index outages; personalization is optional.
Atomic index swaps; bounded staleness is acceptable.

say: Restate it: instant top completions for a prefix, ranked by popularity, at huge QPS.
List functional (suggest, rank, freshness, typos) and non-functional (latency, QPS, freshness, eventual consistency).
Name the forces: read-heavy, latency-critical prefix lookup with skewed traffic.
Size QPS, latency budget, vocabulary, and how few suggestions you return.
Sketch GET /suggest (cacheable) and the query-event ingest.
Start naive to expose the latency problem, then a trie, then precomputed top-k.
Add edge caching for the skew, then stream-driven rebuilds for freshness.
Go deep where pushed: top-k build, freshness/rebuild, or fuzzy matching.
Shard by prefix with replicas; let the cache absorb the hot head; balance skew.
Summarize requirements and name the dominant tension (freshness vs read-path simplicity).

expectations: Reaches for a trie for prefix lookup instead of a DB scan.
Knows results should be ranked by popularity and reads dominate.
Precomputes top-k per node and caches the skewed hot head with TTLs.
Drives freshness via stream aggregation and atomic rebuilds.
Keeps all mutation off the 1M-QPS read path via offline rebuild + atomic swap.
Shards by prefix with replicas and handles letter-frequency skew.

followUps: How would you support multi-word / mid-phrase completion rather than just prefix-of-the-whole-string?
How do you internationalize for languages without spaces or with large alphabets?
How would you filter unsafe or offensive suggestions and prevent manipulation of popularity?
How do you A/B test ranking changes without disrupting the live read path?
How would you support entity-aware suggestions (people, products) beyond raw query strings?
How do you handle a sudden viral spike for a brand-new term faster than the rebuild interval?
